findings.location: “general-ai — [scope flag]”; “Contribution / population flags”; “Never put flags in facets.”
findings.issue: The vocabulary calls general-ai, steering, non-developer, and general-code flags, while the output schema and adjudicated answers place them in facets and reserve flags for disposition. This invites structurally different JSON outputs.
findings.test_case: A paper proposes general-LLM governance and substantially uses fine-tuning. One tagger puts general-ai and steering in facets; another follows the word “flag” and puts them in flags.
findings.proposed_fix: Rename all four to “scope/contribution facets” everywhere. State: “The JSON flags array is reserved exclusively for demote:context or demote:discard.”

findings.location: “from its FULL TEXT below”; PART 3 labels four records “TITLE+ABSTRACT ONLY”
findings.issue: The task promises full text, but four known-answer cases provide only sparse metadata. Tags such as built-system, framework, lit-review, mode, and oversight-explanation often cannot be established from an abstract.
findings.test_case: An abstract says “we present a framework” without saying whether it was implemented, and the unavailable body describes a working prototype. A contents-only tagger must omit built-system, while the gold answer includes it.
findings.proposed_fix: Require full text for calibration and production, or add an explicit insufficient-evidence state and prohibit scoring absent-evidence tags as errors on abstract-only records.

findings.location: “Steering-only solution → demote candidate”; “PRIMARY (one per paper)… same tail as the membership tag”
findings.issue: A steering-only paper can correctly receive no theme, yet the schema still requires a primary corresponding to a membership theme. The struggle rule says to demote and move on but does not resolve the impossible primary.
findings.test_case: A paper contributes only a prompt-optimization method and no inspection, comprehension, or gating mechanism. It must be demoted and receive no solution theme, but the output still requires a primary theme.
findings.proposed_fix: Allow primary_theme:null when a demoted paper has no valid theme, and require a primary to be a member of themes only when themes is nonempty.

findings.location: “include demote:context ONLY”; M74M3RFJ gold flag: “already demoted core->context 2026-07-15”
findings.issue: The known answer contains a value forbidden by the operative schema, so no compliant tagger can reproduce the exact adjudicated flag set.
findings.test_case: A context paper was demoted during an earlier pass. One tagger emits demote:context as required; another copies an audit-note string from the gold data, producing incompatible outputs.
findings.proposed_fix: Normalize M74M3RFJ to flags:["demote:context"] and store historical notes outside the tagging schema.

findings.location: “demote:context… any reason”; “include… ONLY if the paper looks mis-scoped… general-AI, general-code, tool benchmark, secondary lit, not operationalizable”
findings.issue: Reading A: every listed category presumptively requires demotion. Reading B: they are merely discretionary warning signs, as “looks mis-scoped” and “candidate” imply. F9JM9CI6 demonstrates that general-code can remain core.
findings.test_case: A vision paper proposes a reusable AI reviewer for ordinary human-written code and directly addresses augmenting human review. One tagger demotes it because it is general-code; another keeps it core because the mechanism transfers centrally.
findings.proposed_fix: Provide a binding disposition matrix: identify automatic demotions, rebuttable presumptions, and audit-only facets, with one positive and negative example for each.

findings.location: “risk-routing… which/whether/when AI items reach a human”; “hitl-workflow… HOW + WHEN the human acts”
findings.issue: Reading A assigns checkpoint timing to risk-routing because it determines when the human is engaged. Reading B assigns it to hitl-workflow because checkpoint placement is when the human acts. Both definitions explicitly claim “when.”
findings.test_case: An agent requests approval before database writes but not before read-only actions; every item is subject to this fixed policy. One tagger calls the action-dependent timing risk-routing, while another calls it checkpoint placement under hitl-workflow.
findings.proposed_fix: Define risk-routing as item selection, priority, or tier before engagement; define hitl-workflow timing as fixed or selected checkpoint placement after an item is assigned to human control.

findings.location: PART 2: “Gate on a computed, producer-independent signal… model self-confidence is disqualified”; PART 1: “signal + selection/tiering logic”
findings.issue: The producer-independence requirement and self-confidence exclusion are absent from the operative copy. A tagger can therefore assign risk-routing to autonomy decisions based solely on the producing agent’s confidence.
findings.test_case: A coding agent silently merges when its own confidence exceeds 0.9 and otherwise asks a human. Part 1 permits risk-routing; Part 2 disqualifies the signal.
findings.proposed_fix: Add to Part 1: “Routing requires a computed signal independent of the producing model; producer self-confidence alone does not qualify.”

findings.location: PART 2: “expects dual-tagging on gray-zone systems… ‘LLM writes tests then runs them’” and “oracle’s independence”; PART 1 only separates probabilistic AI judgment from deterministic grounded checks
findings.issue: Part 1 omits the dual-tagging rule and the independent-oracle caveat. Hybrid systems can consequently receive ai-review only, rules-based-checks only, or both without a shared decision procedure.
findings.test_case: An LLM creates tests, executes them, and interprets failures to accept code. One tagger tags ai-review because the LLM controls the judgment; another tags rules-based-checks because executable tests produce the verdict.
findings.proposed_fix: Add the gray-zone composition rule and state that execution earns rules-based-checks while LLM interpretation earns ai-review; record AI-authored-oracle circularity in the rationale.

findings.location: PART 2 tooling-supply-chain boundary: “Excludes keyword false-positives (hardware trojans, classic SolarWinds-class incidents)”; no equivalent in PART 1
findings.issue: The operative definition lacks the explicit wrong-object exclusion, despite tooling-supply-chain being especially vulnerable to keyword matching.
findings.test_case: A paper surveys SolarWinds and hardware-trojan detection but never discusses AI coding tools or attacks on AI reviewers. Part 1’s broad provenance/vetting language can permit the tag; Part 2 excludes it.
findings.proposed_fix: Add: “Exclude conventional software/hardware supply-chain security unless the governed object is AI coding tooling or the attacked object is the AI oversight layer.”

findings.location: PART 2 framework: “one-off point tool” excluded and “Breadth is NOT the criterion”; PART 1 excludes only “a bare taxonomy/decision-model”
findings.issue: Part 1 materially weakens the point-tool boundary, making any implemented integratable tool defensibly a framework. This affects Lumen and vision architectures.
findings.test_case: Authors release a single-purpose IDE dependency viewer with no reusable architecture or pipeline design. One tagger calls it an integratable technical framework; Part 2 classifies it as a point tool.
findings.proposed_fix: Carry over the adoption test verbatim: reusable pipeline design qualifies regardless of breadth; a one-off algorithm, benchmark, measurement, or point tool does not.

findings.location: “survey-input — adoption / preference / RAI-priority finding useful for the org survey design”
findings.issue: Reading A includes any such finding, including statistics synthesized by a literature review. Reading B requires participant or organizational evidence produced by the paper. “Useful” supplies no reproducible threshold.
findings.test_case: A narrative review repeats five vendor surveys reporting Copilot adoption and desired controls but gathers no new data. One tagger applies survey-input because the findings inform question design; another omits it as secondhand evidence.
findings.proposed_fix: State that survey-input requires original empirical participant/organization data; secondary papers may point to source studies but do not receive the facet unless secondary evidence is intentionally allowed.

findings.location: Global rule says “membership, not mention”; only ai-review and steering explicitly mention “documented practice”
findings.issue: There is no general rule for when an empirical description of practitioners’ existing behavior earns a solution theme. A practice can substantively contribute to hitl-workflow, routing, or explanation without proposing a mechanism.
findings.test_case: An ethnography finds that developers establish mandatory approval checkpoints and revoke agent permissions after risky edits. It proposes no tool, leaving no rule on whether the documented control practice earns hitl-workflow.
findings.proposed_fix: Add: “A documented practice earns the corresponding solution theme when it is analyzed as a substantive finding or recommendation; incidental workflow description does not.”

findings.location: “intro-framing — position / agenda / definitional paper that names the gap”
findings.issue: Reading A restricts the facet to papers whose genre and main contribution are position, agenda, or definition. Reading B applies it to any empirical or review paper that explicitly names a gap. The adjudicated 2CKL96B8 combination shows the distinction matters.
findings.test_case: A literature review synthesizes vulnerability evidence and ends with a governance research agenda. One tagger applies intro-framing because it names the gap; another omits it because the paper is primarily a review.
findings.proposed_fix: State that the facet requires a paper-level agenda or definitional contribution, but may co-occur with lit-review when that agenda is an explicit contribution rather than routine future-work prose.

findings.location: “ai-code-insecurity — empirical security-vulnerability evidence in AI code”
findings.issue: Reading A requires observational or comparative evidence that AI-generated code actually contains vulnerabilities. Reading B includes evaluation of an AI-specific vulnerability taxonomy on seeded projects. The latter is needed to force VibeGuard’s adjudicated membership.
findings.test_case: A security gate detects seeded source-map and secret leaks in synthetic vibe-coded projects but does not estimate real-world prevalence or compare AI with human code. One tagger applies ai-code-insecurity; another treats it solely as detector evaluation.
findings.proposed_fix: Explicitly include empirical characterization or evaluation of security failure modes claimed to be distinctive to AI-generated code, including seeded evaluations; exclude generic detector benchmarks with no AI-code risk claim.

findings.location: “If lit-review applies, the primary MUST be the biggest-tent theme”
findings.issue: Reading A chooses the theme covering the most cited studies or sections. Reading B chooses the broadest conceptual category even if supported by less evidence. “Biggest-tent” supplies no measurable selection rule.
findings.test_case: A review devotes half its evidence to AI-code security, one quarter to quality, and one quarter to AI-review remedies, while its conclusion frames review reliability as the overarching issue. Competent taggers can choose ai-code-insecurity or ai-review as primary.
findings.proposed_fix: Define the primary as the theme stated in the review objective/conclusion and supported across the largest share of synthesized evidence; if those tests disagree, use the evidence-share winner and document the tie-break.

findings.issue: The schema does not say whether rationales are required for themes only, every facet, the primary, or demotion. Different key sets can all appear compliant.
findings.test_case: A paper receives three themes, four facets, and demote:context. One tagger supplies rationales only for themes; another supplies seven tag rationales plus a demotion rationale.
findings.proposed_fix: Require one rationale for every assigned theme and facet, plus demotion when present; state that primary reuses its membership-theme rationale and needs no duplicate key.

known_answer_analysis.weak_rules: Part 1 omits the one-off point-tool exclusion for framework, so Lumen can defensibly receive framework despite the gold omission.
known_answer_analysis.suggestion: Restore the full framework adoption test and explicitly classify a single-purpose context viewer without reusable pipeline architecture as a point tool.

known_answer_analysis.weak_rules: The instrument expressly recognizes documented practice for ai-review and steering but not for hitl-workflow, and “agentic” still requires an inferential review-unit decision.
known_answer_analysis.suggestion: Add the global documented-practice rule and state that professional use of agents delivering task/artifact-level changes counts as agentic even when humans initiate tasks.

known_answer_analysis.weak_rules: The plumbing rule helps exclude tests, but “argues about” remains subjective; the primary rule does not uniquely rank verification against the paper’s self-organizing and asynchronous-oversight innovations.
known_answer_analysis.suggestion: Operationalize membership as a research question, evaluated contribution, or conclusion-level claim, and choose primary by the main evaluated claim when several innovations are presented.

known_answer_analysis.weak_rules: The remediation exclusion is decisive, but ai-code-insecurity does not say whether seeded evaluation of AI-specific failure modes counts as empirical vulnerability evidence.
known_answer_analysis.suggestion: Add the seeded AI-specific failure-mode inclusion and state that detector policy levels without human allocation do not constitute risk-routing.

known_answer_analysis.weak_rules: “Approval gates” permits hitl-workflow for ordinary human review, while “reference architecture integratable into a pipeline” permits framework; both are absent from the gold. The primary can also be read as human-control novelty from the title.
known_answer_analysis.suggestion: Exclude unchanged ordinary reviewer authority from hitl-workflow, set a concrete architecture-detail threshold for framework, and say reviewer-agent novelty makes ai-review primary when human retention is framing rather than a new control mechanism.

known_answer_analysis.weak_rules: Demotion is discretionary; “tool benchmark” is only an example of something that may look mis-scoped.
known_answer_analysis.suggestion: Make isolated benchmark studies of reviewer-model reliability a stated context-tier rule, or remove demote:context from the mandatory known answer.

known_answer_analysis.weak_rules: Its adjudicated flag is illegal under the schema. Intro-framing is genre-ambiguous, and layered guardrails can defensibly attract solution themes without a sharper plumbing/contribution test.
known_answer_analysis.suggestion: Normalize the flag to demote:context; clarify intro-framing and require a mechanism to be an evaluated or conclusion-level claim before assigning a solution theme.

known_answer_analysis.weak_rules: The supplied abstract is truncated and does not establish lit-review, assistive mode, the three memberships, or why ai-review is the biggest-tent primary.
known_answer_analysis.suggestion: Supply full text or mark the record insufficient-evidence and exclude it from exact known-answer scoring; also replace “biggest-tent” with an operational primary rule.

known_answer_analysis.weak_rules: The abstract supports routing, human control, agentic mode, and survey evidence, but does not establish a working implementation, reusable architecture, or decision-ready explanation. Scope-drift wording may also permit an extra tag.
known_answer_analysis.suggestion: Provide full text for exact scoring; never infer built-system, framework, or oversight-explanation absent explicit evidence, and distinguish unfamiliar-task routing from intent-departure detection.

overall: Not ready for a 148-paper sweep. Several calibrated boundaries are now strong, but namespace confusion, impossible demoted-primary cases, discretionary disposition rules, overlapping “when” definitions, and material compression gaps will still produce systematic divergence. Normalize the schema and gold flags, restore the omitted routing/detector/framework boundaries, define practice-based membership and primary selection, and require full text—or an insufficient-evidence protocol—before production tagging.
